summary: Bulgarian-French husband-and-wife duo known for monumental environmental art installations involving wrapping landmarks in fabric (empaquetage). Major works include the Wrapped Reichstag, Running Fence, The Gates in Central Park, The Umbrellas, and Surrounded Islands. Part of the Nouveau Réalisme movement. They flew in separate planes so one could continue their work if the other died.

works.name: Wrapped Reichstag (1995)
works.description: Christo and Jeanne-Claude wrapped the German Reichstag parliament building in Berlin with over 100,000 square meters of polypropylene fabric fastened with blue rope. The Reichstag's glass dome was later designed by Norman Foster. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Wrapped the German Reichstag parliament building in fabric/polypropylene
examples: This husband-and-wife duo designed large landmark projects such as the Wrapped Reichstag.
Running Fence is a large-scale work using this material by Christo and Jeanne-Claude, who also used this material to wrap the Reichstag.
Christo and Jeanne-Claude once wrapped this building in polypropylene.

clues.clue: Blue rope was used to fasten over 100,000 square meters of fabric
examples: Blue rope was used to fasten over 100,000 square meters of fabric in a 'wrapping' of a building in this city by Christo and Jeanne-Claude.

clues.clue: The Reichstag wrapping took place in Berlin in 1995
examples: One of the structures Christo and Jeanne-Claude wrapped up was this Berlin building in 1995.

works.name: Running Fence (1976)
works.description: A 24.5-mile-long fence of white nylon panels supported by steel cables, installed in Sonoma and Marin counties, California. It was totally disassembled after 14 days. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Over 24 miles of nylon installed in California
examples: Over 24 miles of nylon were installed in this state for Christo and Jeanne-Claude's Running Fence project.
A 24.5-mile-long work made of this material in California used steel cables to support its panels.

clues.clue: A 'Running' fence in the Bay Area comprised of nylon sheets, disassembled after 14 days
examples: Christo and Jeanne-Claude created a 'Running' one of these structures in the Bay Area which was comprised of nylon sheets, and was totally disassembled after 14 days.

works.name: The Gates (2005)
works.description: Over 7,500 saffron/bright orange vinyl gates installed along pathways in Central Park, New York City. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Over 7,500 saffron-colored/bright orange gates installed in Central Park
examples: This location was the site of an art installation by Christo and Jeanne-Claude that featured over seventy-five hundred gates.
Christo and Jeanne-Claude put their landmark The Gates, a series of 7503 bright orange gates, along paths in this New York City park.
A team of artists who installed saffron-colored gates in Central Park

works.name: The Umbrellas (1991)
works.description: Simultaneous installation of thousands of umbrellas in Japan (1,340 blue) and California (1,760 yellow). The project was dismantled early after one umbrella toppled and killed a person. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Thousands of umbrellas installed in Japan (blue) and California (yellow); removed after one toppled and killed a person
examples: Another project by Christo and Jeanne-Claude was the installation of thousands of these objects in Japan and California. 1,340 blue ones were installed in Japan while 1,760 yellow ones were installed in California. They had to removed when one toppled and killed a person.

works.name: Surrounded Islands (1983)
works.description: Pink polypropylene fabric was used to extend the perimeter of islands in Biscayne Bay, Miami. Forty tons of garbage were removed as part of the project. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Extended the perimeter of islands in Biscayne Bay with pink material; forty tons of garbage removed
examples: Some forty tons of garbage were removed as part of a project to extend the perimeter of islands in Biscayne Bay with material of this color in an early '80s project by Christo and Jeanne-Claude.

works.name: Wrapped Coast (1969)
works.description: 1.5 miles of Australia's Little Bay coastline wrapped in fabric for ten weeks, making it the largest artwork in the world at the time. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Wrapped 1.5 miles of Australia's coastline (Little Bay) in 1969; was the largest artwork in the world
examples: Christo and Jeanne-Claude wrapped 1.5 miles of this country's coastline in 1969.
For ten weeks in 1969, the largest artwork in the world was a 1.5-mile stretch of Australia's Little Bay that had been modified in this way.

works.name: Wrapped Pont-Neuf (1985)
works.description: The Pont-Neuf bridge in Paris was wrapped in golden sandstone-colored fabric. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Wrapped the Pont-Neuf bridge in Paris
examples: They've done this to landmarks such as the Pont-neuf and this German parliament building.
This environmental artist who frequently collaborated with her husband Christo wrapped landmarks like the Reichstag, the Pont-Neuf bridge, and the Miami islands in brightly colored cloth.

comprehensive_summary:
Christo and Jeanne-Claude are a husband-and-wife artistic duo best known for monumental temporary environmental installations involving the wrapping of landmarks and landscapes in fabric, a practice they called empaquetage. They were part of the Nouveau Réalisme movement founded by Yves Klein.

Their most frequently clued work is the Wrapped Reichstag (1995), in which they covered the German parliament building in Berlin with over 100,000 square meters of polypropylene fabric fastened with blue rope. This is essentially a giveaway-level clue that appears in nearly every question about the duo. The Gates (2005), an installation of over 7,500 saffron-colored vinyl gates along pathways in Central Park, New York, is the second most commonly referenced work. Running Fence (1976), a 24.5-mile-long nylon fence in California that was disassembled after just 14 days, also appears frequently.

Less commonly clued but still important works include The Umbrellas (1991), a simultaneous installation of blue umbrellas in Japan and yellow umbrellas in California that was tragically dismantled early after one toppled and killed a person; Surrounded Islands (1983), which extended the perimeter of Biscayne Bay islands with pink fabric after removing forty tons of garbage; Wrapped Coast (1969), where they wrapped 1.5 miles of Australia's Little Bay coastline, making it the largest artwork in the world at the time; and the Wrapped Pont-Neuf (1985) bridge in Paris.

Biographical clues to note: they flew in separate planes so one could continue their work if the other died, and initially only Christo received credit for their joint work. They are frequently compared to Robert Smithson and other land/earth artists. Questions about their work often appear not as direct answerlines but as clues within tossups about locations (Berlin, California, Central Park) or materials (fabric, fences), making cross-referencing essential.
